content.text: Agents that hallucinate through failures cause more damage than agents that stop clearly. Refusals and fallbacks are the engineering discipline of making 'I cannot do that' a first-class, structured, routable event rather than an unhandled crash or a confusing partial success.

A refusal is a response in which the agent explicitly declines to perform the requested action, explains why, and provides a user-facing message. A fallback is what the agent offers instead: cached data, a simplified alternative, a human handoff path, or a modified retry. Together, refusals and fallbacks define the agent's
behaviour — how it behaves when it cannot comply with a request, whether because of a policy constraint, a capability limit, a missing precondition, or a tool failure.

Without explicit refusal and fallback design, agents in production behave unpredictably under stress: they silently return empty results when tools fail, hallucinate plausible-sounding answers when they lack the required information, or produce responses that look like success to the calling code but are actually failures in substance. Structured refusals prevent all three by making non-compliance explicit, typed, and machine-readable.

The consequences of unstructured failure differ from the consequences of structured refusal. An unstructured failure — a missing field, a silent empty result, a confusingly hedged prose response — propagates invisibly into downstream stages of a pipeline, where it either crashes with a cryptic error far from the original failure point, or produces silently wrong results that compound through further processing. A structured refusal, by contrast, is caught at the point of generation, routed to the appropriate handler, and either resolved or escalated before it can corrupt downstream state.

Consider a financial agent that is instructed to execute a wire transfer but lacks the required authorisation level for the requested amount. Without explicit refusal logic, the model might: attempt the transfer and fail at the API level (with an uninformative error), return a partial result that the calling code interprets as success, or hallucinate a confirmation that the transfer was completed. With explicit refusal logic, the model returns a structured
that the calling code routes to a human approval queue.
Structured refusals stop cascading failures. The pipeline can distinguish a refusal from a success and take the appropriate action at each stage.
A clear explanation of why an action was not taken — and what the user can do instead — maintains trust even in failure cases.
Machine-readable refusal metadata is loggable and queryable. Refusal rates by reason code are a diagnostic metric for system health.
Explicit policy refusals prevent the model from being manipulated into performing prohibited actions. A policy refusal is more reliable than hoping the model's training generalises correctly.
When an agent cannot proceed, the system's behaviour is as predictable as when it can — testable, loggable, routable.
An agent that refuses to answer rather than guesses eliminates the most harmful class of errors in high-stakes domains.
Structured refusals with reason codes enable routing to human reviewers, alternative systems, or cached responses without human judgment at the routing layer.
A graceful fallback that returns cached data within the SLA window is often better than a failure that violates the SLA.
Systems that handle failure gracefully accumulate trust over time; systems that fail silently lose trust in sudden, unpredictable ways.
Prompts that define refusal criteria too broadly cause the model to refuse legitimate requests, frustrating users and reducing the system's utility.
If refusals are too common, users learn to work around them (prompt injection attempts, paraphrasing the request) rather than accepting the refusal gracefully.
Fallbacks are by definition lower quality than the primary response. If the fallback is too degraded (e.g., 'please contact support'), it may be worse than no response at all.
Automatic retry with a modified prompt adds latency and token cost. For high-volume pipelines, the retry rate must be managed through prompt quality.
The refusal schema must be kept in sync across the system prompt, the Pydantic model, the parser, and the routing logic. Changes to one require updates to all.
Refusals fall into three types, each with a different appropriate handling strategy:

The agent is capable of the action but is not permitted to perform it given its instructions or the user's context. Example: an agent refusing to reveal system configuration, or declining to process a request that violates a content policy. Policy refusals should be final — no retry.

The agent cannot perform the action because required information is missing, a precondition is unmet, or the request is outside the model's knowledge. Example: refusing to retrieve a record when the record ID is absent. Capability refusals should trigger clarification or escalation, not a final refusal.

The agent attempted to fulfil the request but a tool call failed — timeout, rate limit, API error, partial success. These should trigger retry logic with exponential backoff, and after max retries, a graceful fallback with a human-readable explanation.

A critical discipline is keeping these types separate in the structured output. A policy refusal and a tool failure look similar to the user (both result in the agent not fulfilling the request), but they require entirely different handling by the system: policy refusals must not be retried, while tool failures must.
All responses — success and refusal alike — should share a top-level schema with a
field that drives routing. The refusal-specific fields encode the reason code, user-facing message, and recommended next action:

{
  "status": "refused",
  "reason": "policy | capability | tool_failure | out_of_scope",
  "user_message": "Human-readable explanation for the user",
  "action": "stop | ask_user | retry | escalate | fallback",
  "fallback_data": null,
  "retry_after_seconds": null
}
When the agent refuses a request, the fallback strategy determines what happens next. The appropriate strategy depends on the refusal type and the system's capabilities:
Return the last known good response from a cache. Appropriate when the data is not highly time-sensitive and the tool failure is transient.
Return a lower-fidelity answer that the agent can produce without the failing tool. Example: return order status from a database read instead of a real-time logistics API.
Route the request to a human agent with full context included. Appropriate for high-stakes operations or ambiguous refusal cases.
Retry the request with a modified prompt that addresses the failure cause. Appropriate for capability refusals where the missing information can be inferred or requested.
Explain what happened and what the user can do next, then stop. Appropriate for policy refusals and cases where no good fallback exists.

The diagram below shows the full refusal and fallback flow. Every LLM response passes through a refusal detector. Success responses flow directly to the output; refused responses enter the fallback router which applies the appropriate strategy by refusal type, with a retry arc for tool failures.

attrs.alt: Refusal detection and fallback flow: LLM response goes to refusal detector, success flows to output, refusals go to fallback router (policy→stop, capability→ask user, tool failure→retry with backoff), retry arc goes back to LLM call

content.text: When to Use Explicit Refusal and Fallback Design
The agent performs irreversible actions (financial transactions, data deletion, account changes).
The agent's output feeds automated downstream processes without human review.
The system has SLA requirements that must be met even when the primary path fails.
The deployment is public-facing and adversarial inputs are expected.
Regulatory or compliance requirements mandate auditability of non-compliance events.
Internal tools where users have context to interpret failures themselves.
Exploration systems where an occasional unhelpful response is acceptable.
Prototypes where the refusal logic would add more complexity than value.
Specify the structure of all possible refusal responses before writing the system prompt. Use a Pydantic model with a discriminated union or a shared base with refusal-type-specific fields.
List every condition under which each refusal type should fire. Be specific: 'refuse if order amount exceeds $10,000 and user_role != manager' is testable; 'refuse if the request seems risky' is not.
Add explicit refusal rules to the system prompt:
Use conditional language: 'If X, return {status: refused, reason: policy, ...}'. Include the exact structure of the expected refusal response.
Write application-layer code that reads the
fields from the response and dispatches to the appropriate handler.
Write eval cases for each refusal trigger:
For every refusal rule, write a test case with an input that should trigger the refusal and assert the expected
Implement retry with backoff for tool failures:
Tool failure refusals should trigger automatic retry with exponential backoff. After max retries, escalate to a human or return a cached fallback.
Log all refusals with reason codes:
Instrument the response parser to count refusals by reason code and alert when rates exceed expected thresholds.
Example 1: Structured Refusal with Tool Use

import anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Literal, Optional

class AgentResponse(BaseModel):
    status: Literal["ok", "refused", "error"]
    reason: Optional[Literal["policy", "capability", "tool_failure", "out_of_scope"]] = None
    action: Literal["complete", "stop", "ask_user", "retry", "escalate"]
    result: Optional[str] = None
    user_message: Optional[str] = None
    missing_fields: Optional[list[str]] = None

TOOL = {
    "name": "respond",
    "description": "Submit the agent response.",
    "input_schema": AgentResponse.model_json_schema(),
}

SYSTEM = """
You are a financial operations agent. You can execute wire transfers
for authorised users with amounts up to $50,000.

REFUSAL RULES:
1. If the user's role is not 'manager' or 'admin' and amount > $10,000:
   return {status: "refused", reason: "policy",
           action: "escalate",
           user_message: "Transfers over $10,000 require manager approval."}

2. If the transfer amount or recipient account is not specified:
   return {status: "refused", reason: "capability",
           action: "ask_user",
           user_message: "Please provide the transfer amount and recipient account.",
           missing_fields: ["amount", "recipient_account"]}

3. If the banking API call fails:
   return {status: "refused", reason: "tool_failure",
           action: "retry",
           user_message: "The banking system is temporarily unavailable. Retrying."}

In all other cases, process the transfer and return:
{status: "ok", action: "complete", result: "Transfer of $X to account Y initiated."}
"""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def process_transfer(user_message: str, user_role: str) -> AgentResponse:
    context = f"User role: {user_role}\nRequest: {user_message}"
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        temperature=0,
        tools=[TOOL],
        tool_choice={"type": "any"},
        system=SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": context}],
    )
    block = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    return AgentResponse.model_validate(block.input)

# Test cases
result = process_transfer("Transfer $50,000 to account ACC-9876", user_role="analyst")
print(result.model_dump_json(indent=2))
# Expected: status=refused, reason=policy, action=escalate

import time, logging
from enum import Enum
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional, Callable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ActionCode(str, Enum):
    COMPLETE = "complete"
    STOP = "stop"
    ASK_USER = "ask_user"
    RETRY = "retry"
    ESCALATE = "escalate"

class AgentResponse(BaseModel):
    status: str
    action: ActionCode
    reason: Optional[str] = None
    result: Optional[str] = None
    user_message: Optional[str] = None

def with_fallback(
    call_agent: Callable[[], AgentResponse],
    get_cached: Callable[[], Optional[str]],
    escalate_fn: Callable[[AgentResponse], None],
    max_retries: int = 3,
) -> AgentResponse:
    """
    Wraps an agent call with a full fallback router:
    - success: return immediately
    - policy refusal: stop, return refusal
    - capability refusal: return refusal with clarification prompt
    - tool failure: retry with exponential backoff, then cached data or escalate
    """
    last_response = None
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        response = call_agent()
        last_response = response

        if response.status == "ok":
            return response

        if response.reason == "policy" or response.reason == "out_of_scope":
            # Policy refusals: never retry
            logger.info("Policy refusal: %s", response.reason)
            return response

        if response.reason == "capability":
            # Capability refusal: return clarification, no retry
            logger.info("Capability refusal — missing: %s", response.user_message)
            return response

        if response.reason == "tool_failure":
            if attempt < max_retries - 1:
                delay = 2 ** attempt  # exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s
                logger.warning("Tool failure on attempt %d, retrying in %ds", attempt + 1, delay)
                time.sleep(delay)
                continue
            # Max retries exceeded: try cached fallback
            cached = get_cached()
            if cached:
                logger.warning("Using cached fallback after %d tool failures", max_retries)
                return AgentResponse(
                    status="ok",
                    action=ActionCode.COMPLETE,
                    result=f"[cached] {cached}",
                    user_message="Showing last known data — live data temporarily unavailable.",
                )
            # No cache: escalate
            logger.error("No cache available, escalating after %d failures", max_retries)
            escalate_fn(response)
            return AgentResponse(
                status="refused",
                action=ActionCode.ESCALATE,
                reason="tool_failure",
                user_message="This has been escalated to our support team.",
            )

    return last_response  # Should not reach here
Medical Information Agent with Multi-Level Refusals

A healthcare platform deploys an agent that answers patient questions about medications. The agent must refuse to provide specific dosage advice (policy — clinical liability), acknowledge when it lacks information about a specific drug interaction (capability), and handle timeouts to the drug database gracefully (tool failure). Without structured refusals, the prototype returned fabricated dosage information for drugs it didn't have data on (hallucination on capability gap) and crashed when the drug database was slow (unhandled tool failure).

After implementing three-tier structured refusals, all fabricated responses were eliminated: the model explicitly refused for dosage questions (returning a policy refusal with a message directing users to a pharmacist), returned structured capability refusals for unknown drug combinations (with the drug names recorded for manual database entry), and used cached drug data for tool failures. The refusal rate by reason code became a live health metric for the system.

Routing all refusals to the same handler — a generic 'something went wrong' message — discards the reason code that distinguishes fixable situations from permanent ones. A capability refusal (fixable with more information) should trigger a clarification flow; a policy refusal (permanent) should explain the limit and stop; a tool failure (transient) should trigger retry.
A refusal that returns only a machine-readable status code leaves the application responsible for generating a user-facing explanation. Always include a
field in the refusal response so the explanation is generated in context, close to the model's understanding of why it is refusing.
Separate policy, capability, and tool failure reasons:
Each requires different handling; conflating them makes routing impossible.
Generate the user-facing explanation in the model's context, where it has access to the reason for refusal.
Returning a success status when an action failed corrupts downstream state and breaks trust more severely than a clear refusal.
Implement exponential backoff for tool failures:
Immediate retry on a rate-limited or overloaded API worsens the situation. Back off at 1s, 2s, 4s intervals with jitter.
Log refusal reason codes as metrics:
Refusal rates by reason are leading indicators of system health — a spike in tool_failure refusals signals infrastructure issues before they affect the user-facing error rate.
For each rule in the system prompt, write an eval case that triggers that specific refusal and asserts the expected reason and action codes.
Offer a concrete alternative in refusals:
Every refusal should answer 'what can the user do instead?' — even if the answer is 'contact support at X'.

Structured refusals add two forms of overhead: the schema tokens in the system prompt and the fallback handler logic in the application layer. The schema overhead is fixed and acceptable. The fallback logic overhead is proportional to the refusal rate — at 2% refusal rate with retry logic, the average latency per request increases by approximately 2% × retry_latency.

For tool failure refusals, pre-warm caches for high-traffic data so that cached fallbacks are available immediately when needed. Measure cache hit rates for fallback requests separately from normal requests — a fallback that always misses the cache provides no reliability benefit.

Define the unified response schema (success + refusal) as a single tool with a discriminated union or a shared schema with optional refusal fields. All responses are schema-validated at the API level.
to get typed access to both in Python.
to tool call wrappers for automatic retry with backoff.
Trace full message chains including refusal responses. Track refusal rates by reason code as dashboard metrics.
Emit refusal reason codes as custom metrics. Set up alerts on threshold violations for tool_failure and policy reason codes.
Policy refusals are a security mechanism, not just a user experience feature. When a model is instructed to refuse specific request types and to return a structured refusal with a

reason code, the refusal is testable and auditable. You can verify in an eval suite that the model refuses correctly, and you can verify in production logs that policy refusals are firing at the expected rate. Untested policy compliance — relying on the model's training to refuse appropriately — is not auditable and not reliable enough for high-stakes deployments.

For systems where refusal accuracy is a safety requirement, add a separate lightweight guard model that reviews the main model's response before it is returned. The guard model checks for compliance with policy refusal requirements and escalates any response where the main model should have refused but did not. This two-model pattern catches cases where the main model hallucinates through a policy refusal.

Think of refusals and fallbacks as the flight crew's emergency procedures. On a normal flight, the procedures are never needed. But every crew member knows exactly what to do in every emergency scenario — which actions to take in which order, which communications to send, which fallback systems to activate. They practise these procedures so that under stress, the response is automatic and structured. An LLM agent without explicit refusal and fallback design is a crew that has only practised normal flight; it will improvise in an emergency, and improvised emergency responses are unpredictable.
Refusals fall into three types — policy, capability, and tool failure — each requiring a different handling strategy; never treat them the same.
Use a shared response schema for successes and refusals; parse both identically and route by the
Never fake success on tool failure; returning a success status when an action failed corrupts downstream state and destroys trust.
Only retry tool failure refusals with exponential backoff; policy and capability refusals must not be retried.
field in refusals — generated in-context, close to the model's understanding of the refusal reason.
Log refusal reason codes as metrics and alert on unexpected spikes — they are leading indicators of system health problems.
Test every policy refusal rule with an explicit eval case; untested policy compliance is not reliable enough for production.
For high-stakes deployments, add a lightweight guard model that verifies the main model refused when it should have.
